title: Battle of Yarmouk
summary: Over six days in mid-August 636, on a broken plateau above the Yarmouk River south-east of the Sea of Galilee, a Rashidun Arab army led by Khalid ibn al-Walid destroyed the main Byzantine field force of the emperor Heraclius. The battle broke Roman power in Syria after nearly seven centuries and opened the Levant to permanent Muslim rule. It ranks with Actium and Manzikert among the most decisive engagements in Mediterranean history.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The 630s were an extraordinary moment of geopolitical vertigo. In 628 Heraclius, emperor at Constantinople since 610, had ended a twenty-six-year war with the Sasanian empire by marching to the outskirts of Ctesiphon and forcing a Persian peace. Two years later he had personally carried the True Cross back into Jerusalem. Yet both empires were exhausted, their treasuries drained, their armies unpaid, their frontier client kingdoms neglected. When Muhammad died at Medina in June 632 the Arabian tribes he had unified began raiding across the desert into both empires. Under the caliph Abu Bakr, and after his death in 634 under Umar ibn al-Khattab, these raids became a coordinated campaign of conquest.

Syria was the natural first target. Arab forces from the Hijaz slipped past the old Ghassanid frontier without serious resistance; in July or August 634 they broke a Byzantine army at Ajnadayn in southern Palestine, and in September 635 they occupied Damascus after a six-month siege. Emperor Heraclius, now in his sixties and afflicted with the swelling illness that would kill him, based himself at Antioch and Edessa and set out to raise the largest army the empire could still afford. The command was entrusted to the Armenian general Vahan, sacellarius Theodore Trithyrius, and a mixed force of Armenians, Byzantines, Christian Arabs under the Ghassanid phylarch Jabala ibn al-Ayham, and possibly Slavs and Franks in imperial pay.

How many soldiers each side put in the field is one of the oldest unresolved questions of early Islamic history. Arab chroniclers, writing in the ninth century and later, gave the Byzantine army figures between 100,000 and 240,000 and their own at 24,000 to 40,000. Modern historians, notably Walter Kaegi and David Nicolle, consider these ratios propaganda and estimate a Byzantine force of perhaps 15,000 to 40,000 and a Muslim force of similar or slightly smaller size. Warren Treadgold, working from Byzantine tax and pay records, has argued for a somewhat higher Byzantine total of around 80,000. Almost all agree the Byzantines were numerically superior but by nothing like the ten-to-one margin of legend.

In May 636 Heraclius sent his army south from Antioch. Faced with converging Byzantine columns, the Arab commanders wisely evacuated Damascus, Emesa and other newly won towns and concentrated their forces at Jabiya on the Golan. Command in the field was consolidated in Khalid ibn al-Walid, the aggressive general who had led the celebrated march from Iraq across the Syrian desert two years earlier and who was already legendary among the tribes as Sayf Allah, the Sword of God. Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah, the caliph's formal appointee, generously ceded tactical command to him for the coming battle.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: Terrain and dispositions of the Battle of Yarmouk, August 636.

sections.body:
The two armies faced each other for weeks along the plateau north of the Yarmouk gorge, a country of dry ravines and volcanic ridges rising above the deep river valley that today marks the border between Jordan and Syria. Skirmishing, negotiation and single combats dragged on through July as Vahan probed for weakness and Khalid manoeuvred to deny him room. The Byzantine army was uneasy: pay was short, the Armenian and Ghassanid contingents distrusted one another, and rumours of Persian collapse in Iraq undermined morale.

The main battle opened on Tuesday 15 August 636 and lasted, in most reconstructions, six days. Vahan attempted a broad frontal assault to pin the Arab centre while flanking columns rolled up their wings. On the first days the Byzantine attacks pressed the Arab right hard; the women of the camp, according to al-Waqidi and Ibn Ishaq, seized tent poles and drove wavering warriors back into the line, an episode Muslim tradition remembers vividly. Khalid held four cavalry squadrons in reserve and used them repeatedly to plug gaps, exploiting the fact that his opponent had no mobile reserve of comparable weight.

On the fourth day, remembered as the Day of Lost Eyes because so many Byzantine archers pierced Arab shields at close range, Vahan's foederati of Christian Arabs began to defect. On the fifth day Vahan sought a truce, which Khalid refused. On the sixth day, Monday 20 August, Khalid launched a decisive cavalry envelopment. The Muslim horse, screened by dust storms out of the south-east that blew grit into Byzantine faces, wheeled around the imperial rear and cut the road to Damascus. The Byzantine line broke and, in the chaos of retreat, was pushed into the labyrinth of gorges above the Yarmouk. Thousands were driven over the cliffs at Wadi ar-Ruqqad or hunted down in the ravines. Theodore Trithyrius was killed; the Ghassanid Jabala fled to Anatolia and eventually to Constantinople; Vahan is said to have died in the pursuit. Muslim casualties, though heavy in some accounts, allowed the army to march on Damascus within days.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: Gold solidus of Emperor Heraclius, whose reign ended with the loss of Syria after Yarmouk.
image.credit: Classical Numismatic Group, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
Heraclius, waiting for news at Antioch, is said to have bidden a bitter farewell to Syria as he sailed north: Salaam Suriya, wa ni'ma al-baladu hadhi lil-'aduw, "peace to Syria, and what a fine land this is for the enemy." There was no reserve army left with which to defend the interior of the province. Damascus fell for the second and final time in late 636; Jerusalem, after a two-year siege, capitulated to Umar in person in 638 on terms that guaranteed the safety of Christian holy places; Antioch, Aleppo and the coastal cities followed. Within four years the Levant east of the Taurus mountains had been permanently detached from the Roman world.

The strategic effects rippled outward. Persia, having lost the battle of al-Qadisiyya against another Arab army in late 636 or 637, could no longer hold Mesopotamia; Ctesiphon fell in 637 and the Sasanian empire disintegrated over the following decade. Egypt was invaded by Amr ibn al-As in 639 to 642 and quickly overrun, with Alexandria surrendering in 641. The whole southern shore of the Mediterranean, from Cilicia to Cyrenaica, was on its way to being Arab and Muslim, and with it the fiscal base of the Byzantine state was slashed by more than half.

Inside the newly conquered lands the immediate impact was gentler than the battle might suggest. The Muslim conquerors were a small elite settled in new garrison cities, or amsar, such as Basra and Kufa in Iraq and later Ramla and Kairouan. Christian and Jewish communities kept their laws and clergy under a poll tax, and Greek continued to be an administrative language until the reforms of Abd al-Malik at the end of the seventh century. Local elites, especially in Syria, adapted quickly, and the Umayyad caliphs who ruled from Damascus after 661 built their new religious architecture, most famously the Dome of the Rock in 691, on foundations that a Byzantine engineer would have recognised.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The battle of Yarmouk stands at the head of the tradition of early Muslim military historiography. Khalid ibn al-Walid, dismissed by the caliph Umar shortly after the victory to prevent his prestige from eclipsing that of the state, became the paradigmatic Arab general, and every subsequent Muslim commander from Musa ibn Nusayr to Salah al-Din was measured against him. The battle's tactical shape, of an outnumbered Muslim force using speed, cohesion and cavalry envelopment to shatter a heavier but brittler Roman army, entered the repertoire of Islamic military manuals and, through Ottoman and Mamluk transmission, of later Middle Eastern warfare.

For Byzantium the wound was catastrophic. The empire that emerged from the seventh century was smaller, poorer and more Anatolian, its army reorganised into the theme system and its cultural identity narrowed. The loss of Syria and Egypt severed it from the Miaphysite Christian communities whose theological quarrels had dogged Constantinople for two centuries, but also from the grain and revenues that had underwritten late Roman civilisation. The great cities of the eastern Mediterranean, Antioch, Alexandria, Caesarea Maritima, would never again be Roman.

Modern historiography continues to debate the precise numbers, the sequence of manoeuvres and even the identity of some Byzantine commanders, and the primary Arab sources are notoriously prone to rhetorical exaggeration. The disputed casualty figures alone range across an order of magnitude. But there is no serious disagreement about the outcome or its meaning. In six August days on the Yarmouk plateau, the political map of the Mediterranean world was redrawn, and the Roman era of the Near East, running back through Diocletian and Trajan to Pompey the Great, was brought to a definitive end.

worldContext.text: In the Frankish kingdoms Dagobert I, the last effective Merovingian king, was ruling from Paris; his death in 639 would open the long period of mayor-of-the-palace dominance that led eventually to the Carolingians. In Anglo-Saxon Britain the pagan Mercian king Penda was extending his power in the Midlands, while the Northumbrian court of King Edwin, converted to Christianity by Paulinus in 627, had already been broken at Hatfield Chase in 633. Visigothic Spain under the pious Chintila continued to legislate against its Jewish minority.

In China the Tang Emperor Taizong was at the height of his powers, having crushed the Eastern Turkic khaganate in 630 and welcomed the returning Buddhist pilgrim Xuanzang from India in 645. In 636 his beloved empress Zhangsun died and he composed for her the celebrated grief-stricken memorial poems. In Japan the Soga clan still dominated the Yamato court, but the reforming Prince Naka no Oe would overthrow them within a decade in the Isshi incident of 645.

Northern India was still ruled by the emperor Harsha of Kannauj, whose religious assemblies at Kanauj and Prayag were attended by the Chinese pilgrim Xuanzang around this time. Harsha would die without an heir in 647, ending an unusually large post-Gupta consolidation. In the Deccan the Chalukyas under Pulakeshin II were extending southwards, and along the Silk Road the Western Turks were losing their control over the Sogdian cities to the surging Tang.

The Christian kingdom of Aksum had entered its long decline, and the trade of the Red Sea was slipping from Aksumite into Arab hands. In Nubia the united kingdom of Makuria under King Qalidurut would famously repulse two Arab invasions in the 640s and 650s, forcing the caliphate into the Baqt trade treaty that preserved Christian rule along the middle Nile for six more centuries. West of the Nile the Berber confederations of the Sahara were largely unaware that a new empire was about to reach them.

In Mesoamerica the great Maya cities of the southern lowlands were at their late Classic zenith. Palenque was ruled by K'inich Janaab Pakal, now well into his long reign, and Tikal and Calakmul were pursuing their long dynastic rivalry through client kings and set-piece wars. In the central Andes the highland empire of Wari was consolidating its administrative centres from Ayacucho, while Tiwanaku on the Bolivian altiplano continued to project ideological influence far beyond its own borders.

figures.name: Khalid ibn al-Walid
figures.role: Arab general who commanded the Rashidun army at Yarmouk; nicknamed Sayf Allah, the Sword of God

figures.name: Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah
figures.role: Companion of the Prophet and formal Muslim commander in Syria; ceded tactical command at Yarmouk to Khalid

figures.name: Vahan
figures.role: Armenian general and Byzantine field commander, sent by Heraclius to recover Syria

figures.name: Heraclius
figures.role: Byzantine emperor from 610 to 641; supervised the campaign from Antioch and abandoned Syria after Yarmouk

figures.name: Jabala ibn al-Ayham
figures.role: Last Ghassanid Arab phylarch, commanding Christian Arab foederati in the Byzantine army; defected and later fled to Anatolia

images.caption: A modern reconstruction of the terrain and troop dispositions of the Battle of Yarmouk in August 636, drawing on Kaegi and Syvänne.
The expansion of the caliphate under Muhammad, the Rashidun and the Umayyads, 622 to 750. Yarmouk opened the western arm of the conquests.

sources.title: Muslim Conquest of Persia and Egypt
